name: XLA and StableHLO
summary: TensorFlow XLA: an MLIR-based compiler for tensor computations targeting CPUs, GPUs, TPUs. StableHLO: the portable, versioned operator set that frontend frameworks (JAX, PyTorch via torch.export, TensorFlow, ONNX) target as a stable interchange. SPIR-V's role for GPU shaders, applied to ML compilers.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra)** is TensorFlow's compiler for tensor computations: takes a graph of tensor operations and emits optimised code for CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs. Originally tightly coupled to TensorFlow, XLA was rebuilt on MLIR and decoupled into the OpenXLA project.

**StableHLO** is the portable successor to XLA's HLO: a versioned, MLIR-defined operator set that frontend frameworks (JAX, PyTorch via `torch.export`, TensorFlow, ONNX) can target as a stable interchange format, decoupling them from XLA backend evolution. StableHLO is to AI compilers what SPIR-V is to GPU compilers: a portable IR that lets frontend and backend evolve independently.

sections.title: StableHLO design
sections.content:
The op set is small (~150 ops), each with a precise spec covering shape inference, broadcasting semantics, and numerical behaviour. Operations are tensor-shaped: most are element-wise or reduction operations on multi-dimensional arrays, with broadcasting rules that the spec defines precisely.

Status (as of 2026-04): StableHLO is the OpenXLA-blessed exchange format, with `torch.export` to StableHLO emerging as the canonical PyTorch-to-XLA path; JAX produces StableHLO directly via `jax.export`. The frontend-decoupling matters for ecosystem health: framework releases can ship with their preferred StableHLO version, and backend releases can support a range of versions, without coordinating timelines.

sections.title: Lesson — versioned IR for ecosystem evolution
sections.content:
The lesson generalises beyond AI: **a domain with rapid hardware evolution and many frontend frameworks needs a stable mid-level IR with versioned operator semantics**, separating frontend concerns (autodiff, dynamic shapes, model authorship) from backend concerns (codegen, scheduling, kernel fusion, hardware targeting).

The split mirrors LLVM IR's role for general-purpose compilation but is specialised for tensor-graph workloads and shaped by the AI hardware diversity (NVIDIA, AMD, Apple, Google TPU, AWS Trainium, Cerebras, Groq) that no single backend can serve well.
